critique.summary: The article describes Hypura, a software tool that enables running very large language models (LLMs) on Apple Silicon Macs with limited memory by streaming model tensors from NVMe storage. It explains how Hypura optimizes tensor placement across GPU, RAM, and NVMe tiers to allow models larger than physical memory to run without crashing, demonstrating performance benchmarks on models like Mixtral 8x7B and Llama 70B.
mainPoints: Hypura is a storage-tier-aware inference scheduler that places model tensors across GPU, RAM, and NVMe based on access patterns and hardware capabilities.
It enables running models larger than the Mac's physical memory by streaming tensors on demand from NVMe storage, avoiding crashes caused by out-of-memory errors.
Hypura supports different streaming modes: full-resident for smaller models, expert-streaming for Mixture of Experts (MoE) models, and dense FFN-streaming for large dense models.
Benchmarks on a 32 GB M1 Max Mac show Hypura running a 31 GB Mixtral 8x7B model at 2.2 tokens/second and a 40 GB Llama 70B model at 0.3 tokens/second, where naive approaches crash.
The software profiles hardware capabilities and automatically optimizes tensor placement and prefetching without manual tuning.
Hypura exposes an Ollama-compatible HTTP API for integration with other tools and is open source, built with Rust and CMake.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article are consistent with known technical principles of memory management and model inference on constrained hardware. The approach of streaming tensors from NVMe storage to extend usable memory is plausible and aligns with existing research and engineering practices in LLM deployment. The benchmarks provided are specific and realistic for the hardware described (Apple M1 Max with 32 GB unified memory and NVMe storage). The repository is publicly available on GitHub, which supports transparency and reproducibility. However, the article does not cite independent third-party evaluations or multiple sources, relying primarily on the author's own benchmarks and explanations.
considerations: The performance metrics depend heavily on the specific hardware configuration, including NVMe bandwidth and GPU capabilities, which may vary across different Mac models.
The streaming approach introduces latency and throughput trade-offs compared to fully in-memory models, which may affect real-time applications.
Long-term SSD wear is addressed, but users should consider the impact of sustained high I/O workloads on their specific NVMe drives.
The software requires building from source with Rust and CMake, which may limit accessibility for less technical users until a prebuilt package or Homebrew tap is available.
The article does not provide comparisons with alternative solutions or commercial products that might offer similar capabilities.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the technical challenges and innovations of running large language models (LLMs) on Apple Silicon, focusing on memory bandwidth, model sizes, and inference speed. Commenters debate the practicality of current approaches, the impact of memory tiers, and the handling of sparse MoE models with respect to NVMe storage and OS paging.

comments.author: anshulbasia27
comments.text:
OS paging would be significantly worse here. The kernel's page fault handler is reactive — it doesn't know you're about to read layer 47's FFN weights, so it can't prefetch. You stall on every fault, wait for the 4KB/16KB page to load, then resume. With 80 layers of dense FFN streaming, that's thousands of cold faults per token.

  What makes this approach faster is that the model's access pattern is completely deterministic during         
  inference. You know exactly which tensors are needed next because transformer layers execute sequentially. So
  you can issue large sequential reads and prefetch the next layer while the current one is computing on Metal. 
  The OS page cache can't do that — it has no concept of "layer N+1 comes after layer N."

  For MoE it's even more stark. The OS would page in all 8 experts on the first token that routes to each one,  
  then evict them under memory pressure with LRU, which has no idea that expert 3 fires 10x more often than
  expert 7. The neuron cache here is basically a domain-specific replacement policy.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate explanation of why the specialized caching and prefetching strategy outperforms default OS paging, especially for deterministic transformer layer access patterns.

comments.author: vicchenai
comments.text:
the practical question is whether the read pattern is sequential enough to actually saturate nvme bandwidth or if the attention layer access pattern ends up being random enough to kill throughput. sequential reads on a decent nvme get you 5-7 GB/s, random reads drop to maybe 500 MB/s depending on queue depth.

for a 1T model youd need to stream something like 2TB of weights per forward pass at fp16. even at peak sequential thats 300+ seconds per token which is... not great for interactive use but maybe fine for batch inference where you dont care about latency.

still a cool proof of concept though. the gap between 'can run' and 'runs usefully' is where things get interesting.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly highlights the bandwidth bottleneck and the difference between sequential and random reads on NVMe, providing a realistic perspective on performance limitations.

comments.author: shubhamintech
comments.text: The MoE point matters here ie sparse activation means you're not reading all 2TB per forward pass, but the access pattern flips from sequential to random which is exactly the worst case for NVMe. Been thinking about this a lot for agent inference workloads where you want consistent latency more than peak throughput.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately notes the tradeoff in MoE models between reduced data volume and increased random access, which negatively impacts NVMe throughput.

comments.author: msbhogavi
comments.text:
"As much memory as possible" is right for model capacity but misses bandwidth. Apple Silicon has distinct tiers: M4 Pro at 273 GB/s, M4 Max at 546 GB/s, M4 Ultra at 819 GB/s. Bandwidth determines tok/s once the model fits in memory. An M4 Max gives you 2x the decode speed of an M4 Pro on the same model.

For what Hypura does, the Max is the sweet spot. 64GB loads a 70B at Q4 with room to spare, and double the bandwidth of the Pro means generation is actually usable instead of just technically possible.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly emphasizes the importance of memory bandwidth over just capacity in determining inference speed on Apple Silicon hardware.

comments.author: EnPissant
comments.text:
You do not provide any comparison to llama.cpp with mmap.

You do not explain how any kind of predictor can work for MoE experts.

You do not explain how prediction can even be useful. I can predict the layers used in a dense model (all of them are used in order), but that doesn't help me much. It's still bottlenecked on bandwidth (hint: MoE doesn't change this).
comments.analysis: This comment raises valid points about missing comparisons and the limited benefit of prediction in overcoming bandwidth constraints, especially for MoE models.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text:
This is <1 tok/s for the 40GB model.

Come on, "Run" is not the right word. "Crawl" is.

Headlines like that are misleading.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a critical perspective on marketing language, accurately pointing out that very low token speeds do not constitute practical running.

comments.author: Insanity
comments.text:
This is a pretty cool project! Essentially this is like using Swap memory to extend your RAM, but in a 'smart' way so you don't overload the NVMe unnecessarily.

I do wonder in practice how the 'smarts' pan out, because putting a ton of stress on your NVMe during generation is probably not the best choice for it's longevity.
comments.analysis: The comment thoughtfully considers the tradeoffs of the approach, including potential hardware wear from intensive NVMe usage.

comments.author: simonw
comments.text:
Suggestion for the maintainers: the comparison table currently lists some pretty old models, Qwen 2.5 14B and Mixtral 8x7B and Llama 3.3 70B.

A lot of people are reporting incredible results with the Qwen 3.5 MoE models on Apple hardware right now (streaming experts - see https://simonwillison.net/2026/Mar/24/streaming-experts/) - it would be great to get some of those models into that table.

Maybe the 1T parameter Kimi K2.5 too if you can get that to work, see https://twitter.com/seikixtc/status/2036246162936910322 and https://twitter.com/danpacary/status/2036480556045836603
comments.analysis: A constructive suggestion to improve the project's relevance by including newer and more capable models in benchmarks.

comments.author: astrange
comments.text:
> Consumer hardware (MacBook Pro, Mac Studio) ships with fast unified memory and NVMe storage, but limited capacity. A 32 GB M1 Max cannot naively load a 40 GB model — the OS will swap-thrash until the OOM killer intervenes.

macOS doesn't have an "OOM killer" in that sense. (It has an out of swap space killer but it's pretty weak.)

So what will happen is, either your memory wiring will fail, or else it will get really slow and panic.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately clarifies macOS memory management behavior, correcting a common misconception.

comments.author: baq
comments.text: Intel Optane rolling in its grave.
comments.analysis: A humorous but uninformative comment that does not contribute meaningful technical insight.
